sentences.text: Michael Steinstock.
Michael S-Z-T-A-J-N-S-Z-T-O-K.
Sztajnsztok.
I'm born in Poland, Lublin.
Poland I don't have to spell.
L-U-B-L-I-N
Lublin.
May the 23rd, 1919.
I come from a very orthodox family.
My father is a tailor and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a very poor man.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I have two sisters.
The first sister, the name is Serl, and the second name-- husband-- is Wisnia, W-I-S-N-I-A.
The second sister is Sara, called here Sonya.
The second name, from the husband, is Herscman-- H-E-R-S-C-M-A-N, Hercman.
Rywka-- W-- R-Y-W-K-A.
The second name is R-A-J-Z-L-A, Rajzla.
I'm the youngster.
I live in a one kitchen, no-- no windows [LAUGHS] in one room.
When-- when mine middle sister get married, she lived with her husband in the room.
I, my father, and mine mother lived in the kitchen.
In the same kitchen, you cook, and sleep, and everything-- no windows, and no water, and no toilet.
It's apartment, a very old-- about 150 year old.
No, no.
In this-- no, a lot of families there-- not my family.
Now, a lot of families are there.
About 10 families.
And I live of the street is the name Ruska-- R-U-S-K-A, Number 30, 3-0.
Oh, yeah.
I remember everything.
Only Jewish.
This is-- I live in a neighborhood-- 500 years ago is there a ghetto.
And this was by the King Borislav [INAUDIBLE].
And this [INAUDIBLE] come like this-- about 500 years the same way.
The people lived there-- Jewish people lived in this neighborhood.
And Lublin-- in lived about 40,000 Jew.
Yeah, a nice city.
And--
No, the life was normal.
And the people-- the Jewish people had a lot of crops, you know, growing crops.
And they had unions, Jewish unions.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, they have a theater, Jewish theater.
No, my mother-- when I'm born, my mother 40 years old.
And she gets sick of asthma.
Asthma.
And my father's a very poor man, and they cannot afford to go somewhere.
You understand what I mean?
I remember [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a lot of days I get up in the morning, no bread.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, my older sister had five sons-- the older sister.
And she moved to Warsaw in 1930.
She moved to Warsaw with her husband, with their five sons.
The other sister lived together with my mother and father and with me.
And she have a twin, a boy and a girl.
And then, by Hitler, she born a other baby, a-- a son.
When I'm not in the home, I'm in Russia at this time.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] What I can tell you-- my brother-in-law was live together with-- with my parents, and worked with my father to get a tailor.
No, work for somebody, you know?
And my father work only three months in the year.
That's it.
In the season.
In the season, you know.
Tailor.
This is he worked for the stores what saved clothes for wintertime and for spring.
Summer time, nothing to do.
Very orthodox.
Kosher-- this is normal.
My father has the special uniform.
A special black suit with a special hat.
My mother wear a-- a wig, a sheitel.
Jewish--
I saw her, yeah.
Natural, I saw her.
I'm the son.
I can see.
No, she had blonde hair.
She's a very tall woman.
I'm very happy my father is five foot, a little man and skinny-- a very skinny man.
And my mother is tall and the-- no.
And-- and the people-- and my father and mother loved our-- our-- one or the other-- a very nice couple.
Nobody there can help you.
But the-- the people's in the same situation.
No, the city helped you.
In winter time, get you coal, because you warm up the house with the oven-- get you coal.
And you know the blackness?
It helped you a little, the welfare.
Yeah.
The [? joint ?] helped a little.
Yeah.
I went to school number 28, a public school.
And this school is 21 [?
Samonska ?] Street.
Yeah.
And I walk 3 kilometers on a one side to the school.
Alone.
Alone.
Now, once you got to school, were you mostly with Jewish children?
Only.
Because before the war, is only boys-- no girls in this school, and Jewish boys.
Girls at a different school.
Before the war, boys and girls doesn't go in a one school-- separate.
Polish.
I go to the school to 14 years.
Because seven grade-- only seven grade, the public school.
I went [? there ?] a yeshiva.
After school.
I have long [? payot. ?] I'm very orthodox.
I learned the religion-- religion, you know, the Bible.
No, like normal, like here-- the same thing.
Oh, yeah.
And there is the-- in Lublin, you have the biggest yeshiva in Europe.
It's called Chachmei, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeshiva Chachmei Lublin.
This mean in Hebrew, the Smart Yeshiva in Lublin-- in English.
And this built in 1928, they built the yeshiva.
[INAUDIBLE] and make like and built this in the same, like, in Israel, 2,000 years ago, in the [INAUDIBLE], by [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know in English how to--
Yeah.
At the same day, for every student there are a room-- no luxury.

And I remember in the house, my mother, my father, my brother talk, as one rich Polish man [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] get down for the pillows what he was [? doing. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the rabbi said-- Rabbi Shapiro-- it was a--
It was a da-- ah, biggest rabbi in Europe.
Shapiro.
I not remember that.
I know in person about-- see, I walked in the street, into the synagogue and said, you not can use the down because the goose is not kosher.
They take the down and sold this and bought kosher down.
How little you know.
And--
Everyday.
My sis--
Nobody talked Hebrew.
My father talked, you know?
This was you learn there, in the yeshiva, or in the special schools-- a little school-- this was old Hebrew.
It's called [NON-ENGLISH].
This was a little different like this Hebrew was in Israel.
This is modern Hebrew in Israel.
And--
No.
No, she in the house, a religion.
And she-- she know-- the sister was living with my parents.
And the sister was lived in Warsaw.
It's not fanatic orthodox.
She is modern orthodox-- modern.
You know, religion.
Don't wear the wigs like my mother.
And my father the same way.
What you mean?
In my town, where I live, is no problems because we lived only Jew.
Sleep Polish people too, only no problems.
Lublin-- maybe other towns who sleep closer to [?
Posnan-- ?] the Germany-- Germany people lived there, Polish Germany people.
It's more trouble, a little, you know?
Yeah, I guess what I'm trying to find out is, when and how did you begin to sense that there was trouble?
Oh.
In 1939, when the Germany come into Lublin-- this was the 17 days of September 1939.
I remember everything.
I remembered the town what I escape and everything.
I could to tell you.
Was it warm weather?
Was it-- what was the weather like?
This was September.
September in Poland is a little cool.
This is in nights, you know?
Oh, it's not over, no, not over.
The holidays is in October.
This is September.
And when they stopped [? here, ?] I remember where I live, everybody is not in the house.
No, it's in the-- every houses have a gate you close.
And everybody looked out.
And at one-- my [? friend, ?] the name is Wasserman.
Somebody told that that the house where his sister live is afire.
And this boy want to go to help there.
And the Germany killed him in the street.
Because nobody can go out.
In one day.
When the German come to first day, when they come in the second day, is the people very, you know, afraid what could happen.
Nobody-- no one knew what was going to happened.
And every calling us from the streets.
You have German with the special machine to kill.
You can't run.
You can walk slowly.
And I--
And I remember one officer, a German officer, come to my father.
He wants to fix the jacket, the [INAUDIBLE].
It's too big of him.
And my father [INAUDIBLE] that's [? mended. ?] And the officer, the German officer, waiting in the house.
And there is my sister's two girls-- and the boy with the girls, the twin.
I take them.
He kissed them and taked out from the pocket, his children from Germany, his children, and cried-- a German officer.
This was-- this was the first week in the occupation.
And I pay my father for the work.
And I said, I don't like the work.
I can do nothing.
I have to go.
Surprised me.
Because before the war, I read the brown book what Hitler writes.
It's called [POLISH] in Polish.
And I find out there what going to happen-- what's happened in German with the Jew.
And I remember somebody tell me that the Russian nine little tanks come to Germany to see him in the city hall, in Lublin.
And I went to see the Russian tank for everybody.
Then [INAUDIBLE] Russian is-- they're having there is everything good.
Wonderful.
And I will see the Russian.
And when I work in center city, the Germany catched me-- a lot of young people and take them in a special place.
This is Lipova 7.
Nobody have idea of got killed to make the concentration camps.
No, people read lot-- before I told you the brown book.
Then the brown book Hitler write out everything what I'm going to do to the Jew.
You understand what I mean?
It's a lot of people no-- a lot of people no believe this!
My mother-- my mother don't can go.
Because she's sick.
She asthma.
My father don't-- father don't go because my mother no go.
My father's, in this time, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] over the 50.
My sister-- she had two little children.
She not can go to Russia.
A lot of people escaped.
In the town, the people-- young people and older people, with children too.
And my brother-in-law don't-- wanted to go, because my sister is with the children.
When I'm-- I take me in the-- on the camp of Lipova 7, I'm there 13 month.
Those are the regular army.
There you're not seeing too-- you not can see too much anti-Semitism.
But the SS, the Gestapo, you know-- and what they told the soldier, the soldier would do, you know?
And other people, it's better [INAUDIBLE] they can get nothing-- kind of bread, and not milk, not-- it's the terrible.
People died in the street.
Right away.
For very hard.
You see, you have a lot-- in this neighborhood, where I live, is a lot of Jewish stores-- little stores, with bread, with herring.
I remember a special store, with the grocery.
The people have a little and sold to you, you know, what I have to-- in the reserve.
No-- very hard to get.
A lot of people go to the farms.
From Lublin to the farms are not so far.
People walked to the farms-- 10 kilometer, 15 kilometer-- bring something for the family.
Yeah.
My family don't talk from this because she-- not-- not possible.
Impossible.
Impossible for him.
My mother-- they can't go.
She's a sick lady.
She stay more on the bed like she walk.
No, the sister was living with my parents.
My one sister with the-- the sons in Warsaw.
And that's it.
I have a lot of [INAUDIBLE] cousins.
I have uncles.
I have a-- aunts.
No, you know, I'm [INAUDIBLE].
Lublin, yeah.
I don't know.
Because when I take me in camp, and I escaped from there-- I escaped from there.
It's impossible to escape.
Two German officers-- it's get cold in winter time.
This is in November, in beginning of November, in 1940.
Comes in two officers, ask, who understand German?
In school, I take German three years.
And I speak German.
Who know if you can to buy wool stocking?
I know, I know.
And take me out.
And this camp is in the center of city from Lublin.
And the two officers who walked-- and I am with him-- no cut the hand, nothing, you know?
And there, in Lublin, no cars.
In '39, you not have cars-- in '40, you didn't have cars in Poland.
And-- I'm just going to stop you here.
We're probably going to continue this on the next reel of tape, OK?
Ah ha.
Maybe you want coffee?
